ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for coconut-milk rice; A0010 (Rice and rice products) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227490003 represents a generic rice dish; no specific code exists for nasi lemak rice in SNOMED CT as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Nasi Lemak rice is approximately 200 grams, providing the fragrant coconut-milk rice base before accompaniments are added.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Lemak (rice only) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron are taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for 'Nasi Lemak (rice only)' with confidence 0.95. Cholesterol (0.0 mg), saturated fat (4.0 g), trans fat (0.05 g), and vitamin C (0.0 mg) were present in the input record attributed to a secondary platform source; saturated fat and trans fat values are retained as plausible for coconut-milk rice and are consistent with the HPB primary data profile. Cholesterol is confirmed zero for a plant-based coconut-milk rice preparation. Vitamin C is confirmed zero as no vitamin-C-bearing ingredients are present.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for 'Nasi Lemak (rice only)' exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 68 is estimated using the closest analogous food: white long-grain rice cooked in water (GI ~64–72, mean ~68) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which is a peer-reviewed journal source. Coconut milk cooking medium does not materially alter the GI of the rice starch. Glycemic load per serving recalculated as (68 × 36.8 g carbohydrate per 100g × 200g serving) / 100 = 49.99, rounded to 49.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Low potassium and saturated fat from coconut milk despite moderate sodium
hypertension.guidance: Although sodium is 172 mg/100g, the very low potassium content and saturated fat from coconut milk undermine DASH-diet benefit; pair with potassium-rich accompaniments such as leafy vegetables and limit portion size to 100–150g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium-high glycemic index of white rice with negligible dietary fibre (0.5g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The medium-high GI (~68) combined with near-zero dietary fibre (0.5g/100g) produces rapid postprandial glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 100g or less and always combine with high-fibre, high-protein accompaniments to blunt glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (4.0g/100g) exceeding typical rice dish levels
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at 4.0g/100g from coconut milk raises LDL-cholesterol risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request reduced-coconut-milk preparation and limit serving size to 100–150g, balancing the meal with unsaturated fat sources.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Lemak (rice only) is the fragrant coconut-milk rice component of the iconic Malay dish nasi lemak, widely eaten across Singapore and Malaysia. Long-grain white rice is simmered or steamed in full-fat coconut milk (santan) with pandan leaves and salt, yielding grains that are soft, slightly sticky, and richly aromatic with a mild coconut sweetness. The dish is almost always served as a base for accompaniments — sambal chilli, fried anchovies (ikan bilis), roasted peanuts, cucumber slices, and a hard-boiled or fried egg — but this record covers the rice component alone. Nutritionally, each 100g provides 190 kcal, 36.8g carbohydrate, 3.4g fat (of which 4.0g is saturated fat from coconut milk), 3.1g protein, and only 0.5g dietary fibre. Sodium is 172mg/100g. The glycemic index is approximately 68 (medium), and a standard 200g serving delivers a glycemic load of approximately 50, which is clinically significant for blood glucose management. The saturated fat content from coconut milk warrants caution for cardiovascular and metabolic health. This dish is halal, plant-based, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is a staple breakfast food in Singapore and Malaysia and is culturally central to Malay cuisine.
common_user_queries: Calories in nasi lemak rice only
Can diabetics eat nasi lemak rice?
How much carbohydrate in nasi lemak rice?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient data are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. GI is estimated from the closest peer-reviewed analogous food (white long-grain rice, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) with clear documentation of the estimation basis.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, mineral panel, B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific nasi lemak rice preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry as of 2026. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — this is a plant-based preparation containing no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no vitamin-C-bearing ingredients are present in plain coconut-milk rice). Glycemic load per serving recalculated at (68 × 36.8 / 100) × 200 = 49.99 ≈ 49.9 for a 200g serving, superseding the input value of 25.0 which appears to have been calculated for a 100g reference portion. FoodEx2 match type corrected to 'closest_parent' as no exact FoodEx2 code exists for coconut-milk rice. SNOMED CT match type corrected to 'closest_parent' as no specific SNOMED CT concept exists for nasi lemak rice.
